children.text: Afro-Peruvian music is one of the most powerful expressions of Black cultural life in Peru. Emerging from the historical experiences of enslaved Africans and their descendants, it brings together rhythm, movement, and voice as forms of cultural continuity and resistance. Although Afro-Peruvian communities have often been marginalized within national narratives, their musical traditions have played a central role in shaping Peruvian culture. Afro-Peruvian music is not only an artistic practice; it is also a space where history, memory, and identity are preserved and reimagined.

At its core, Afro-Peruvian music is deeply rooted in rhythm. During the colonial period, enslaved Africans were frequently prohibited from using traditional instruments. In response, they developed new forms of musical expression using the body, everyday objects, and improvised percussion. These practices evolved over time into distinctive musical traditions that continue to define Afro-Peruvian culture today. Rhythm, therefore, is not only a musical element, it is a historical trace of survival, adaptation, and creativity under conditions of constraint.
Afro-Peruvian music is characterized by a unique set of instruments, many of which reflect this history of innovation and resilience.
One of the most iconic instruments is the

. Cajón is a wooden box drum that the musician sits on and plays by striking its front surface with their hands. It produces a wide range of tones, from deep bass sounds to sharp, snare-like accents. The cajón is believed to have originated during the colonial period, when enslaved Africans adapted shipping crates or wooden boxes into percussion instruments. Today, it is central not only to Afro-Peruvian music but also to other genres, including flamenco.

. The cajita is a small wooden box with a lid, played by opening and closing it rhythmically while striking it with a stick. The resulting sound creates a syncopated, almost conversational rhythm. Historically, the cajita is said to have been adapted from collection boxes used in churches, transformed into a musical instrument through Afro-Peruvian creativity.

The Quijada de burro (donkey jawbone) is perhaps one of the most distinctive instruments. Made from the dried jawbone of a donkey, it produces sound when struck or shaken, causing the loose teeth to rattle. This creates a buzzing, percussive texture that adds depth and complexity to musical ensembles. The quijada reflects the use of natural and available materials to create sound, highlighting the resourcefulness embedded in Afro-Peruvian musical traditions.
Together, these instruments form the rhythmic foundation of Afro-Peruvian music, emphasizing percussion, syncopation, and collective performance.
Afro-Peruvian music includes a variety of musical forms, each with its own history, rhythm, and cultural significance. Among the most well-known are festejo, zamacueca, and landó.

The Festejo is one of the most vibrant and widely recognized Afro-Peruvian genres. Characterized by fast rhythms, strong percussion, and energetic dance, festejo is often associated with celebration and community gatherings. Its lyrics frequently incorporate humor, improvisation, and references to everyday life. The dance that accompanies festejo emphasizes movement of the hips and body, reflecting both joy and embodied expression. At the same time, festejo carries historical significance, as it emerged from Afro-Peruvian communities navigating both oppression and celebration.

The Zamacueca is an older musical and dance form that influenced several genres across Latin America, including the Chilean cueca and the Peruvian marinera. It combines elements of African, Indigenous, and Spanish traditions, making it a key example of cultural mixing. The dance typically involves a courtship dynamic between partners, using handkerchiefs and stylized movement. While zamacueca is often associated with national folklore, its Afro-Peruvian roots highlight the central role of Black communities in shaping Peruvian cultural forms.

The Landó is a slower, more introspective genre. Its rhythms are more subdued, often creating a contemplative or melancholic mood. Landó is sometimes understood as a reconstruction or reinterpretation of older Afro-Peruvian musical traditions, shaped in part by cultural revival movements in the twentieth century. The genre emphasizes depth of rhythm and feeling, allowing space for reflection, memory, and emotional expression.

Afro-Peruvian music is not only about sound. It is also about memory. Through rhythm, lyrics, and performance, these musical traditions carry histories that have often been excluded from official narratives. Songs and dances can reference the past indirectly, preserving fragments of experience that might otherwise be forgotten. In this sense, music functions as a form of oral history, transmitting knowledge across generations.

At the same time, music is a space of resistance. By creating and performing Afro-Peruvian music, communities assert their presence and challenge cultural marginalization. This is especially important in a context where Afro-Peruvian contributions have often been appropriated or minimized within national culture. Musical performance becomes a way of reclaiming identity, visibility, and dignity.

Afro-Peruvian women play a crucial role in these processes. As performers, cultural workers, and community members, they sustain and transform musical traditions. Their participation highlights how music intersects with gender, labor, and identity, reinforcing the idea that cultural expression is deeply embedded in everyday life.
